segments.s259-g81: پس is a temporal/discourse adverb meaning 'then' or 'thereupon'. پشت ایشان is a locative phrase 'behind them' (پشت + pronoun ایشان). سواران جنگ is a noun phrase 'horsemen (سواران) of war', with جنگ as an attributive/genitive specifying what kind of horsemen.
segments.s259-g82: This is a relative/participial clause introduced by کز (that/which). آتش 'fire' and به خنجر 'to the dagger' mark what the color was applied to; ببردند (third‑person plural past tense in poetic register) here means 'they had taken/caused to take (a colour)' and رنگ is the object 'color, hue'. Put together: 'who had caused (their) daggers to take the color of fire' (i.e. their daggers were reddened/fiery).
segments.s259-g83: پس 'then' begins the line; پشت لشکر means 'behind the army/host'. ز پیلان گروه is a poetic way to say 'a group/troop composed of elephants' (ز + noun = 'of/from' indicating composition or source), so 'a troop of elephants.'
segments.s259-g84: زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground'. از پی پیل is an adverbial phrase 'from/in the wake of the elephant(s)' (از + پی = 'because of/after, following'), and گشته ستوه is the predicate: گشته (perfect participle 'become/turned') + ستوه ('weary'), so 'the ground had become weary' (resultative/perfect sense).
segments.s259-g85: درفش خجسته is a noun phrase 'the auspicious/fortunate banner' (noun + adjective). میان سپاه is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the army/host' (میان = middle/among). The order is typical poetic word order: descriptive phrase then location.
segments.s259-g86: ز here means 'from/with' marking material or ornamentation: ز گوهر = 'with jewels'. درفشان is a participial/adjectival form 'shining, glittering.' به کردار ماه is a simile 'in the manner/appearance of the moon' (به = 'as/in the manner of', کردار used poetically for 'bearing/appearance', ماه = 'moon').
segments.s259-g87: ز پیلان introduces cause/source 'because of/from the elephants'. زمین is the subject 'the ground/earth'. سربه‌سر is an adverb 'all over/entirely', and پیلگون is an adjective 'elephant-colored/elephant-like' describing the ground: 'the ground was entirely elephant-colored.'
segments.s259-g88: ز گرد سواران is a causal/agentive phrase 'from the dust (گرد) of the riders' (ز = 'from/of'), هوا is 'the air/atmosphere', and نیلگون is an adjective 'indigo/bluish' describing the air: 'the air was indigo from the riders' dust.' Note گرد here means 'dust' rather than the verbal sense.
segments.s259-g65: سپیده (dawn) is the subject and برآمد is the past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'; ز + noun (کوه) gives the source ('from the mountain') and سیاه is a postpositive adjective modifying کوه. Word order is typical older Persian: subject + verb, then a prepositional phrase indicating origin.
segments.s259-g66: This is a verbless nominal clause (common in Persian poetry) giving the commander’s position: سپهدار ایران is the noun phrase 'the commander of Iran' and به پیش سپاه is a locative phrase 'before/in front of the host' (به + پیش = 'towards/in front of'). The clause implies 'stood' or 'was'.
segments.s259-g67: به آسوده is an adverbial phrase ('calmly/at ease', literally 'with ease') placed before the verb; اسب is the affected noun 'horse' and اندر آورد پای is a poetic order where اندر (in/into) + آورد (brought/set) takes پای ('the foot') as the direct object — together meaning 'set the horse's foot down' or 'brought the horse to rest'. Note the older/pre-classical use of اندر for 'in/into/at'.
segments.s259-g68: یلان را uses را as a topical/definite object marker ('the champions'); به هر سو is a directional phrase 'to/at every side'; همی is the archaic aspectual particle marking continuous/habitual action before the past verb ساخت ('made/formed'), so همی ساخت جای means 'made a place/space' or 'made room' for them in every direction.
segments.s259-g69: سپه را is topicalized with را ('as for the army'); سوی میمنه is the locative 'toward the right (side)'; کوه is the noun being located relative to the army, and بود is the past copula 'was' — overall: 'The army was to the mountain's right.'
segments.s259-g70: ز introduces the source/ground ('from/in respect to the fighting'); جنگ دلیران is 'the battle/fighting of the brave ones'; بی‌اندوه is a predicate adjective 'without sorrow' and بود is the past copula 'was' — together meaning the fighting of the brave was free of sorrow (i.e., they fought cheerfully/bravely).
segments.s259-g71: سوی marks direction 'toward'; میسره modifies رود and here functions as a place-name or qualificative for the river (in context 'the Meisareh stream'); رود آب روان literally 'river, water flowing' places رود and آب together with روان as the adjective 'flowing', so the clause means 'water flowed toward the Meisareh river.'
segments.s259-g72: چنان ... آمد is a demonstrative + past-verb 'so/thus it came' (i.e. it entered in that manner); در خور takes خور in the sense 'cove/bay' (a maritime hollow) so در خور آمد = 'entered the cove'; چو introduces a simile 'as/like', and تن را روان uses روان as 'at ease/in motion' referring to the body — literally 'as the body is (made) flowing/moving'. The line therefore compares the water’s entrance to the movement/relief of a body.
segments.s259-g153: سپهدار پیران is a title + name (Sepahdar Piran); غمی گشت is a nominal phrase + copular verb (غَم + گشت = 'became sorrow/was filled with grief'), where گشت is the past-tense intransitive 'became/turned'. سخت here is an adverbial intensifier modifying the whole clause ('severely/very'), placed after the verb in a poetic/classical order.
segments.s259-g154: برآشفت is a past (perfective) verb meaning 'grew angry/was agitated' with the same subject (the commander) understood. با introduces the cause/agent phrase; تیره modifies خورشید (adjective before noun). خورشید بخت is a genitive/noun + noun cluster 'sun of fortune' (literal) meaning the condition of fortune; the whole prepositional phrase means 'at/by the dark sun (i.e. bleak aspect) of fate.'
segments.s259-g155: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase 'from then on / after that.' نگه کرد is a light-verb construction (نگه + کرد = 'kept watch/kept guard'); جای سپاه is the possessed noun phrase 'the army's place/position' and functions as the object/thing he watches. The verb نگه کرد is in past tense and the order reflects classical Persian flexibility (verb before or after its object in poetry).
segments.s259-g156: نیامدش is a past negative verb with the enclitic pronoun -ش ('to/for him') meaning 'did not come to him' or 'was not granted to him.' بر آرزو is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase 'according to (his) desire' (بر + آرزو), and رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield' which is what 'did not come' — the clause is arranged in a compact, somewhat inverted classical order: verb + indirect object + modifier.
segments.s259-g157: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. آوردگه دید and جای صف are the two negative items joined by و; دید is the past-tense verb 'saw' governing both nouns. Word order is nominal phrase + verb in the compact poetic style.
segments.s259-g158: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning 'kept on / continually'; همی بر زد is a verbal phrase 'kept striking' (بر + زد). از خشم gives the cause 'out of anger.' کف را به کف is an object phrase with the definite object-marker را: 'palm to palm' (i.e. striking/clapping the hands together); the order places the cause and the object after the verb in typical poetic compression.
segments.s259-g159: بر این‌گونه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'on/at/under such circumstances.' کآمد is an archaic/poetic past form 'it came/happened' (impersonal or formal), and ببایست ساخت is a modal construction: ببایست (must/ought) + simple verb ساخت ('to make/prepare') meaning 'one must prepare/make.' The modal precedes the main verb in this classical order.
segments.s259-g160: چو is the conditional/temporal 'when/as.' سوی یلان is 'toward the heroes' (سوی = toward, یلان = heroes). چنگ بایست آخت is idiomatic and contains archaic vocabulary: بایست is the modal 'must,' placed before the main verb آخت, and چنگ is the object being invoked. آخت is an old/poetic verb here whose exact, literal sense is archaic and idiomatic in context; the whole clause means 'when facing the warriors one must do/raise the چنگ,' i.e. perform the named martial action. (Note: آخت is rare/poetic and not transparent in modern usage.)
segments.s259-g113: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past 3rd-person verb meaning 'he commanded/sent'; با is the preposition 'with' linking the following proper name; گیو is the proper name Giv acting as companion/leader; تا before a numeral here means 'as many as / up to' and quantifies the following number.
segments.s259-g114: برفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they went/rode off'; برگستوان‌ور is an adjectival compound meaning 'strong‑armed / mighty' describing the fighters; سوار is the noun 'riders/horsemen' — together the noun phrase 'برگستوان‌ور سوار' is the object/constituent that follows the verb.
segments.s259-g115: سپرد is past 3rd-person singular 'he entrusted/handed over'; آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' (آن = 'then', زمان = 'time'); پشت لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the rear of the army' with پشت ('rear/behind') governing لشکر; the final token بدوی here is the recipient (a name in context) — grammatically the object ('the rear') is stated and the person to whom it was entrusted follows.
segments.s259-g116: که is the relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing a clause describing the person just named; بد جای is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'in an ill/ill-suited post' (بد = 'ill/poor', جای = 'place/post'); گردان پرخاشجوی is a noun phrase 'a troop (گردان) that is aggressive/quarrelsome' with the adjective پرخاشجوی modifying گردان.
segments.s259-g117: برفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they went/rode off'; با is the preposition 'with' marking accompaniment; گیو is the proper name Giv as the person they accompany; جنگاوران is the plural noun 'warriors/fighters' — together 'برفتند با گیو جنگاوران' = 'the warriors went with Giv.'
segments.s259-g118: چو ... و چون ... is a repeated comparative 'as/like X and as/like Y' used to list exemplars; گرگین here is a proper name (Gorgin) rather than the adjective 'wolf-like'; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; the second چون again marks 'like'; زنگه شاوران in this context functions as a personal/tribal name compound (not the literal senses listed) and names another exemplar.
segments.s259-g119: درفشی here means 'standard/bearer of the banner' (the banner or its holder) and is the item/person sent; فرستاد is past 3rd-person singular 'he sent/ dispatched'; و is the conjunction 'and' linking the next noun; سیصد is the numeral 'three hundred' and سوار is 'riders/horsemen' specifying the number and type of troops sent.
segments.s259-g120: نگهبان لشکر is a genitive/attributive phrase meaning 'guards of the army' with نگهبان ('guard') qualifying لشکر ('army'); سوی is the directional preposition 'toward(s)'; رودبار is a place-name or 'river-valley' indicating destination — together 'نگهبان لشکر سوی رودبار' = 'as guards of the army toward Rudbar.'
segments.s259-g33: برفتند is a past plural perfective verb 'they went/set out'. بسته governs کمرها and is a past participial/ adjectival use meaning 'having bound/tied' (girded); Persian often uses such a participle before or after the noun. کمرها is the plural noun 'loins/waists' and here takes the object-role of بسته (i.e. 'their loins girded'). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'for/into battle' indicating purpose.
segments.s259-g34: This short verbless clause lists the fighters' weapons. همه 'everyone/all' is the subject/quantifier; نیزه و تیغ names the weapons ('spear and sword'). هندی qualifies تیغ as 'Indian' (style/origin). به چنگ is an idiomatic locative/comitative phrase meaning 'in hand' or 'held' (چنگ literally 'claw/hand' used figuratively).
segments.s259-g35: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. دانست is past tense 'he knew/learned' with گودرز as its subject (third singular). The clause ends with کآمد سپاه, an inverted reported-clause order where کآمد (an older past form 'it/they came') reports the arrival of سپاه 'the army/host'. This inversion is a poetic/archaic ordering.
segments.s259-g36: بزد is a simple past 'he struck/beat' and takes کوس 'war-drum' as its object: 'he beat the drum'. The second clause 'و آمد' continues with آمد 'he came/set out'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'; زیبد likely names a place (Zibad) rather than the verbal senses listed, and به راه 'onto/the road' indicates direction. Together: 'he beat the drum and departed from Zibad onto the road.'
segments.s259-g37: This clause stacks prepositional and motion elements: ز 'from' + کوه 'mountain' and اندر آمد 'came into' (اندر is an archaic/prepositional 'in/into'). به هامون 'to the hamun/plain' marks the destination, and گذشت is the past 'passed' (they passed onto/through it). The sequence describes descent and crossing from mountain to plain.
segments.s259-g38: کشیدند is past plural 'they drew/drew up' (here 'drew up' in the military sense). It takes لشکر 'the army' as object; the prepositional phrase بر آن پهن دشت 'upon that broad plain' gives the place where they deployed. Note the verb precedes the object in Persian narrative order but is fully grammatical as 'they drew up the army upon that wide plain.'
segments.s259-g39: به کردار کوه is a simile 'in the manner/shape of a mountain' (کردار here = 'manner/appearance'). به introduces the manner ('in the likeness of'). از دو رویه سپاه means 'the army on/from two faces/fronts'—رویه here used as 'surface/face/side' to indicate a two-front formation. Overall this describes the formation: the army in two fronts, resembling a mountain.
segments.s259-g40: This clause describes equipment: ز آهن 'of/from iron' qualifies کلاه 'helmets'. به سر بر نهاده is a compound verbal phrase where نهاده (past participle/verb 'placed/put') takes کلاه as its object and the preceding prepositional elements mark where ('on the head' = به سر بر). The sequence means 'they had placed iron helmets upon (their) heads.'
segments.s259-g137: درفش دلفروز is a noun (درفش = standard/banner) followed by an epithet (دلفروز = “heart‑illuminating” / shining); older Persian poetry commonly places the modifier after the noun. بر پای کرد is a verbal phrase where کرد is the past tense ‘did/placed’ and بر پای کردن here means ‘to plant/fasten on the ground (to set up)’; the subject (he) is omitted as expected in Persian when context provides it.
segments.s259-g138: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark سپه (‘the army’) as definite; به قلب اندرون is a locative phrase (‘into/to the heart of the inner place’), where اندرون = inner quarters and قلب = heart/core. جای کرد is a compound verbal idea (‘placed/stationed’) with کرد as the past tense verb; together the clause means he positioned the army in the innermost place.
segments.s259-g139: سران را همه خواند is literal word order: ‘chiefs (سران) + object marker (را) + all (همه) + خواند’. خواند here functions as ‘called/summoned’ rather than ‘read’; the sequence نزدیک خویش (‘near his own’) places the locative phrase after the verb, with خویش written as خوی + ش (the ش enclitic = ‘his’).
segments.s259-g140: پس پشت شیدوش، و فرهاد پیش is a short, elliptic listing of positions: پس (here adverbial ‘then/at the rear’) + پشت شیدوش (‘behind/Shidūsh’s rear’) followed by a comma and a parallel phrase و فرهاد پیش (‘and Farhad in front’). The main verb is omitted because these phrases describe placement immediately after the previous clause.
segments.s259-g141: به دست چپش is a locative expression where به + دست = ‘at/on the hand’ and چپش = چپ + ش (‘left’ + enclitic ‘his’) = ‘his left (hand)’. رزم‌دیده is an adjective (‘battle‑scarred/veteran’) modifying the following proper name هجیر. The verb is again omitted; this nominal phrase describes who stands at that position.
segments.s259-g142: سوی راست is a locative ‘to/toward the right’ (سوی = toward, راست = right); the following sequence کتماره شیرگیر is a name plus epithet, where شیرگیر (‘lion‑catcher’) is an epithet attached to کتماره. Like the previous segment, no explicit verb is present — it’s a positional label linked to the surrounding clauses.
segments.s259-g143: ببستند is a past plural verb ‘they bound/tied’ (reduplicated stem form for past). ز آهن is an idiomatic poetic instrumental/agent phrase ‘with/from iron’ (ز here = با/از), and به گردش سرای is a prepositional phrase where گردش (rotation/revolving) qualifies سرای (hall/house) — i.e. ‘to/around the revolving hall’. The clause says they fastened/tied (something) with iron to the revolving hall.
segments.s259-g144: پس پشت is ‘rear/behind’ (setting the scene), followed by پیلان جنگی (‘war‑elephants’ — پیلان is plural of پیل ‘elephant’, جنگی = ‘military/warlike’) and به پای (‘at the foot/base’). This is another elliptic locative clause describing forces stationed at the rear; the verb is omitted because the previous clause supplies the action context.
segments.s259-g9: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. آمد is a simple past (3rd sg) 'came/appeared', with پدیدار as its subject 'appearance/manifestation'. از ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'from them' placed after the noun (poetic/archaic order). گناه follows to qualify the appearance (an "apparition of sin" or "sinful apparition"); in classical Persian such qualifiers can follow the noun.
segments.s259-g10: هیونی (object) precedes the verb here (typical Persian flexible word order). برافگند is past tense (3rd sg) meaning 'threw off/let loose/hurled'; the grammatical subject is omitted (understood from the previous clause — the sinful apparition). نزدیک شاه is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'near the king' indicating where the action took place.
segments.s259-g11: که introduces reported content or a clause ('that' / 'for') and here introduces the fact being stated. گودرز و گیو is a coordinated subject. اندر آمد is a compound where اندر (into/within) precedes آمد (past 'came'), giving 'entered/come into'. به جنگ is a directional/complement phrase 'into battle'.
segments.s259-g12: سپه is the subject 'army'. باید is the modal 'must/ought to'. ایدر is an archaic/formal adverb meaning 'here'. م and را are written separately here but function together as the object clitic 'مرا' ('for me') — the tokenization is archaic; م as given does not match the listed senses and is best treated as the enclitic 'me'. بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'without delay/at once', placed at the end to modify the whole clause.
segments.s259-g13: سپاه (army) is the subject and آمد is past 'came'. از نزد is a two-word prepositional unit 'from the presence of/from the camp of' (نزد here = 'in the presence of / at the court of'), followed by the proper name افراسیاب. This is a common classical phrasing 'از نزد X' = 'from X's side/house/camp'.
segments.s259-g14: چو is temporal 'when'. ما بازگشتیم is past plural 'we returned'. بگذاشت is past (3rd sg) 'he let (something) be/he released/put', and آب is its object 'water'. In context this means 'he released/let the water go' (e.g. opened a sluice or let water flow).
segments.s259-g15: کنون is an adverb 'now'. کینه را topicalizes 'hatred' with the object marker را; in effect it yields 'the drum of hatred' (a poetic genitive/qualifier). کوس is 'war-drum' (the thing bound), بر پیل is the prepositional phrase 'on the elephant', and بست is past tense 'bound/fastened'. The clause literally: 'Now he bound the hatred's drum on the elephant.'
segments.s259-g16: همی is a classical adverb here indicating ongoing/characteristic action ('continually' / 'thus'); جنگ ما را is the object 'our battle' (ما را = 'our' / 'for us' with را marking the phrase). کند is the present/habitual form of کردن 'to make/do' used narratively to indicate he 'makes/causes'. پیشدست is an adverb/adjective meaning 'in advance, beforehand, in the vanguard' — so the clause means 'he causes our battle to be put forward / preempts us.'
segments.s259-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' placed at the clause head. گیو is the subject (proper name). اندر آمد is a compound verb (اندر + آمد) meaning 'entered/came in' — اندر gives the directional/inward sense. به پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of his father' (به + پیش + پدر), with پیش here meaning 'in the presence of'.
segments.s259-g2: همی گفت is a narratorial present/pastive construction: همی gives a continuous or iterative flavour ('kept saying'/'was saying') and گفت is the reporting verb 'said'. پاسخ همه is the object phrase 'all the answers' (پاسخ modified by همه). دربه‌در is a predicate/adjectival phrase 'hopeless/desperate' describing the state of those answers; the clause is essentially direct speech or its report.
segments.s259-g3: به گودرز گفت = 'he said/told to Goudarz' with به marking the indirect object/recipient. اندر آور سپاه is an instruction 'bring in/bring together the army' — اندر + آور (bring in) with سپاه as direct object; in narrative the bare stem آور can be read as an imperative/suggestive command.
segments.s259-g4: به جایی که introduces a goal/place clause 'to a place where…'. که heads the relative/subordinate clause. سازی همی رزمگاه is a verbal-nominal construction: سازی (making/preparing) plus همی (presentive/iterative marker) gives the sense 'where (you) will set up/prepare the battlefield' and رزمگاه is the place 'battlefield' governed by that verb phrase.
segments.s259-g5: که introduces a content clause 'that …'. او را uses را as an object/contrast marker attached to the pronoun. همی here is an emphatic/presentive particle giving the clause a firm statement ('indeed/for him there is no…'). آشتی رای نیست is a nominal predicate 'there is no intention/plan of reconciliation' (آشتی رای = 'plan/intent to reconcile', نیست negates it).
segments.s259-g6: به دلش اندرون is a prepositional locative phrase 'in his inner heart' (به + دل + ش + اندرون, where اندرون = inner part). داد را جای نیست is an idiomatic verbless clause 'there is no place for داد' — داد here denotes the idea of giving/forbearance/justice (i.e. yielding or mercy), را marks it as the thing excluded, and جای نیست = 'there is no place/room'.
segments.s259-g7: ز هر گونه با او is an adverbial phrase 'in every manner/way with him' (ز here = 'in/from' in poetic usage, هر گونه = 'every kind/way', با او = 'with him'). سخن راندم is a classical idiom where راندن with سخن means 'I spoke/said' (راندم is perfect/past 'I did'), so the whole segment means 'I spoke to him in every way' — note that راندم is idiomatic and not literally 'drove'.
segments.s259-g8: همه هرچ گفتی = 'everything/whatever you said' (همه modifies the indefinite هرچ; گفتی here is a nominalized/reported 'what you said'). بر او خواندم = 'I recited/told (it) to him' (بر marks the indirect object direction and خواندم is a past verb 'I recited/read/told'). The clause reports that the speaker repeated to that person all that had been said.
segments.s259-g49: This is a nominative copular clause with poetic word order: 'ستاره سنان بود' literally places the predicative noun (ستاره) before the subject (سنان) and finishes with the past copula بود — so: 'the spear (سنان) was a star'. The following 'و خورشید تیغ' parallels that structure with an omitted copula: 'and the sun (خورشید) [was] a blade (تیغ)'.
segments.s259-g50: 'از آهن زمین بود' is a material/source phrase with از marking origin/material ('was of iron from the earth') and بود as the copula referring back to the preceding 'تیغ' (the blade was made of earth's iron). The short poetic connector 'وز' begins the next parallel clause — here functioning like 'و' + 'ز' (and/of) — followed by 'گرز میغ', another nominal phrase where the copula is omitted: 'and the mace [was] a cutting edge.'
segments.s259-g51: بتوفید is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning 'spat forth / flew out' (literally 'they spat'), here used impersonally or referring to missiles/noise emerging. از + noun marks source ('from the cry/voice'); گردان (here 'troop, rank') modifies زمین ('the earth's troops' or 'troops on the ground'). The clause states the origin of what was hurled/issued forth.
segments.s259-g52: ز (poetic variant of 'از') introduces the cause or material: 'from ترگ و سنان' — 'from shreds and spearpoints'. The noun phrase 'آسمان آهنین' is predicative with an omitted copula: 'the sky became iron/steely' (آهنین = made/appearing iron-like). Word order places cause first for emphasis: 'from shreds and spears, the sky [was] iron.'
segments.s259-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; the name گودرز is the subject. The object phrase 'توران سپه را' places the modifier (توران, 'Turanian') before سپه ('host/army') and marks it with the object marker را; بدید is the past tense 'saw'. The whole segment reads: 'When Goudarz saw the Turanian army.'
segments.s259-g54: که introduces the result/continuation clause. 'بر سان دریا' is a comparative/likening phrase ('بر' + 'سان' = 'like/as the sea'); زمین is the grammatical subject and بردمید is the past verb meaning 'carried off / lifted / surged' — so: 'the earth rose/swelled like the sea.' The clause is a vivid simile describing the ground's motion.
segments.s259-g55: This is a repetitive, distributive construction using 'از' to mean 'from/after' in a sequence: 'درفش از درفش' = 'banner from banner' (i.e., banner after banner) and 'گروه از گروه' = 'group from group' (troop after troop). The phrase stresses that units stood together in sequence.
segments.s259-g56: 'گسسته نشد' is a negated past passive/perfect sense: گسسته (broken/separated) + نشد ('did not become' / 'did not happen') = 'were not separated'. The following short clause 'شب برآمد ز کوه' uses برآمد (arose) with ز (from) to state 'night rose from the mountain.' The two parts make a compound statement: no unit separated, and then night came up from the mountain.
segments.s259-g25: که introduces a relative/nominal clause ('which/that'); دل را is the definite object ('the heart' marked by را); ز مهر کسی is a prepositional phrase 'from the love of someone'; برگسل is the verb meaning 'to be/ become severed or loosed (from)', placed at the end as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s259-g26: کجا is a rhetorical 'where/where is there'; نیستش is third-person negative 'does not exist / there is not' with the clitic ش referring back to the subject; با زبان راست is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with a straight (honest) tongue/speech'; دل at the end names the thing being qualified ('a heart'), so literally 'where is there a heart that is not honest in its speech'.
segments.s259-g27: همه scopes 'all' and modifies مهر پیران 'the affection/kindness of the elders' (مهر with the genitive/possessive noun پیران). به ترکان بر است is a copular phrase: بر 'toward/on' + است 'is', meaning 'is directed to / rests upon the Turks' (poetic word order: predicate after the noun phrase).
segments.s259-g28: بشوید here is a verb meaning 'will wash/cleanse' or 'will become clean' (third-person present/future sense in poetry); همی is an adverb 'thus/always'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; از او is 'of/from him'; پاک دست is a verbal adjective phrase 'clean-handed' (i.e. absolved of blame). The clause means the king will figuratively 'wash his hands' of that person and be clean-handed.
segments.s259-g29: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; پیران سپاه bundles 'the elders (pīrān) of the army' or 'the elders, [and] the army' depending on rhythm, with سپاه as the affected group; از کنابد uses کنابد (poetic verb meaning 'to afflict/wear' or 'cause trouble') with از indicating source/relation; براند is a present tense verb 'drive away/expel'. Overall: 'when the elders drive away (the) army' (causal/temporal relation introduced by چو).
segments.s259-g30: به‌روز is an adverbial phrase 'by day' or 'in daylight'; اندرون 'the interior/within' is the locative; روشنایی 'light/brightness' is the theme; نماند is past-negative/poetic present 'does not remain / is left no more'. Together they state that 'by day no light remains within.'
segments.s259-g31: سواران 'horsemen/riders' is the head noun; جوشن (armor) + وران is a compound/epithet 'jushn-varān' meaning 'wearers of cuirasses' (poetic compound often written together as جوشن‌وران); صد هزار is the numeral 'one hundred thousand' placed after for meter. Word order is typical of epic poetry where descriptive epithets and numerals follow the noun.
segments.s259-g32: ز ترکان is a genitive/agentive phrase 'of/from the Turks'; کمربسته is a past-participle/adjective 'girded' meaning 'ready/girt for action'; کارزار 'battle/field' states the purpose or scene. Together: '[they are] girded for battle, of the Turks.'
segments.s259-g129: This phrase is a small clause with the preposition ز (z, “from”) + دیده (dideh, “eye/eyes”) followed by the infinitive/masdar خروشیدن (khurushidan, “to roar / to cry out”) and the finite verb آراستی (ârāsti, 2nd person singular past). Literally ‘from (your) eye a crying you made’ — a poetic word order where the action (آراستی) comes at the end and the masdar names the sound produced.
segments.s259-g130: بگفتی (begofti) is 2nd person singular past ‘you said’; به گودرز is the indirect object ‘to Goudarz’; و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’; برخاستی (barkhāsti) is 2nd person singular past ‘you rose/stood up’. The clause lists actions in sequence with normal S-V order for these verbs.
segments.s259-g131: بدان سان (bedān sān = ‘in that way/thus’) is an adverbial phrase formed from the prefix ب- + دان + سان and sets the manner. بیاراست (biyārast) is a past/singular poetic form ‘he adorned/arrayed’; آن رزمگاه is the direct object ‘that battlefield’. Word order is adverbial phrase → verb → object (poetic).
segments.s259-g132: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ‘which/for’; رزم آرزو کرد is literally ‘battle desire did’ where آرزو (ārzu, ‘desire’) is the object and کرد (kard, ‘did/made’) is the verb — together meaning ‘desired battle’ or ‘longed for combat’; خورشید و ماه (‘sun and moon’) are the nominative agents. The verb کرد is simple past.
segments.s259-g133: چو (cho) here = ‘when/if’; سالار شایسته باشد is a subordinate clause ‘the commander is worthy’ with باشد (bâshad) in the subjunctive/present-tense form used in classical style to state a condition; به جنگ ‘in/for war’ is a locative/complement specifying the context of worthiness.
segments.s259-g134: نترسد (natarasad) is a negative present/future-oriented 3rd singular form ‘does not fear / will not fear’; سپاه is the grammatical subject ‘the army’; از introduces the object/source of fear (‘from’); دلاور نهنگ is a compound epithet meaning ‘mighty/heroic colossus’ — literally ‘valiant whale’ used metaphorically for a great warrior. Word order: verb → subject (poetic inversion) then prepositional phrase.
segments.s259-g135: از آن پس (az ān pas) = ‘after that’; بیامد (biyāmad) is past tense ‘he came’; به سالارگاه is the destination ‘to the hall/seat of the commander’. The phrase places the action in sequence relative to what preceded.
segments.s259-g136: که introduces an explanatory relative clause ‘who/for he’; دارد (dārad) is present tense ‘he has/holds/keeps’; سپه را uses را as the definite object marker (‘the army’ object); ز دشمن نگاه literally ‘from the enemy’s gaze’ where ز = ‘from’ and نگاه = ‘gaze/view’ meaning ‘keeps the army safe/hidden from the enemy’s view’. The clause explains his function.
segments.s259-g105: بیفروز is an imperative-like verb 'set alight/make shine' (here poetic/imperative). لشکرگه is a compound noun 'army‑camp' that is the object of that action. از فر خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from/of his فر (farr, royal/divine glory)', with خویش 'his' expressed by the enclitic ش; word order places the source (از فر خویش) after the place (لشکرگه) for a poetic effect.
segments.s259-g106: سپه را is the definite object marked by را. همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle (marks ongoing or habitual action) attached before دار, which here functions as a verb 'hold/keep' (archaic usage). در بر خویش is an idiomatic locative phrase 'in/within his embrace/side' with the possessive suffix ش on خوی; overall: 'keep the army within/close to him.'
segments.s259-g107: بدان is the imperative of دانستن split here into the prefix ب- and the root دان; it means 'know' or 'be aware'. آبگون is an adjective 'water‑colored/bluish' modifying خنجر 'dagger'. نیو سوز is a binomial attributive phrase: نیو 'new' (dialectal/poetic) + سوز 'burning/heat' — together giving a sense like 'newly burning' or 'fresh‑burning', describing the dagger's quality.
segments.s259-g108: چو here is the simile particle 'as/like'. شیر ژیان is a compound where شیر 'lion' is qualified by ژیان 'living' (i.e. 'a living/dauntless lion'). با Yelan رزم places یلان 'heroes' as the co‑participants (با = 'with') in رزم 'battle'; توز is an archaic/obscure word left untranslated in this line (likely a poetic affix or obsolete noun), retained for sound/metrical effect.
segments.s259-g109: برفتند is the simple past (3rd plural) 'they went/left'. یارانش 'his companions' carries the possessive enclitic ش. با او به هم is a phrase 'with him, together' (با = 'with', به هم = 'together'), so the clause means 'his companions went off together with him.'
segments.s259-g110: ز گردان لشکر is a genitival/partitive phrase 'from the troop/division of the army' (ز = 'from' in poetic register; گردان = 'troop/division', لشکر = 'army'). یکی گستهم is compact: یکی 'one' and گستهم (1st person past 'I wandered/strayed') — 'I, one, wandered' or 'I wandered alone/as one.' The verb گستهم is first‑person past, tying the speaker into the preceding action.
segments.s259-g111: دگر is an adverb 'again' or 'another' setting up a recurrence. گژدهم is a proper name (a person) and the grammatical subject. رزم را uses را to mark the definite object 'the battle', and ناگزیر follows as a predicate/adjectival: 'compelled/forced into (the) battle' — i.e. 'Gozdaham was compelled into battle' (classical word order allows the predicate after the object for emphasis).
segments.s259-g112: فروهل is another proper/name or archaic word introducing the next action. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that' / 'who'), and بگذارد is a 3rd person form meaning 'he lets/allows/causes to pass' (here subjunctive/optative sense 'may let'). از سنگ تیر is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of (a) stone (an) arrow' — in context likely instrumental or descriptive of how an arrow was sent; classical syntax often places such prepositional complements after the verb.
segments.s259-g41: 'برآمد' is a past/resultative verb meaning "arose/issued forth" and introduces the event. 'خروشیدن' is a verbal noun/infinitive (the gerund “roaring”) and combines with the genitive 'کرنای' (of the trumpet) to form the noun phrase "the roaring of the trumpet"; the order is verb + gerund phrase, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s259-g42: 'بجنبد' is a present/imperfect form (with the verbal prefix بـ) meaning "to move/stir" used here with a continuing/emphatic particle 'همی' (marking ongoing or habitual action). 'کوه' is the subject "mountain." 'گفتی' here functions idiomatically as a simile/colloquial particle ("as if / seemed"), not as the ordinary noun/verb senses listed below. 'ز جای' is a simple prepositional phrase "from (its) place." The whole phrase means "the mountain seemed as if it would stir from its place."
segments.s259-g43: The segment begins with the poetic preposition 'ز' ("from/by") plus 'زیبد', here functioning like a noun/participial idea of "beauty/splendour" (an archaic formation). 'همی' again marks continuity/emphasis. 'تا' introduces the resultant/extentive sense "so that / to the point that," and 'کنابد سپاه' places 'کنابد' (a present/formal verb root) with 'سپاه' (the host) as its subject: roughly "the host was thus affected / made to toil or be adorned." Together the clause expresses cause/result: "by their splendour so that the army was (thus)."
segments.s259-g44: 'در و دشت' is a frozen binomial meaning "town(s) and plain(s)"; here 'در' is the older nominal sense related to gate/town rather than the preposition. 'از ایشان' means "from/of them / because of them," indicating cause or source. Adjectives 'کبود و سیاه' follow and describe the resulting colours ("bluish and black"). Word order is noun + prepositional source + adjectives describing resulting state.
segments.s259-g45: This clause opens with the causal/agentive 'ز' ("from/because of") plus 'گرد سپه' where 'گرد' means "dust" (not the adverbial "around"). 'روز روشن' is the noun phrase "bright day," and 'نماند' is a past/resultative verb "did not remain / was no more." The structure expresses that the army's dust made the daylight vanish.
segments.s259-g46: 'ز نیزه هوا' literalizes as "from the spears (and) the air" with 'هوا' meaning the atmosphere/space. 'جز' introduces an exception ("except"), 'به جوشن' places the exception as "for/only armour (cuirasses)," and 'نماند' again means "remained not / was left no more." In context this compactly says the air was so full of spears that nothing remained there except armour.
segments.s259-g47: The opening 'وز' is a compact poetic particle (here functioning like 'و ز' = "and from/by"); it is archaic/contracted and not exactly any modern single sense below. 'آواز اسبان' is "the sound/cry of the horses," and 'و گرد سپاه' adds "and the dust of the host." The whole phrase lists the sources that produce the brightness in the next segment.
segments.s259-g48: 'بشد' here is the aorist/resultative sense "it became / it happened" (not a jussive wish), introducing the resulting noun 'روشنایی' (brightness). 'ز خورشید و ماه' is a simile/source phrase "as if from the sun and moon" — the brightness is compared to sun-and-moon light. Word order is verb (it became) + nominal result + comparative/source phrase.
segments.s259-g121: همیدون (a proper name) is the subject and فرستاد is the simple past verb 'sent'. The prepositional phrase بر سوی کوه ('toward the mountain') follows the verb; بر intensifies the directional meaning of سوی and precedes the destination noun, a typical classical/poetic word order.
segments.s259-g122: This is the direct object(s) of فرستاد: درفشی 'a standard‑bearer / banner' coordinated with 'سیصد ز گردان گروه' — 'three hundred from the ranks of the troop'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', linking the numeral to its source (گردان گروه). The phrase is elliptical: the verb applies to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s259-g123: یکی here introduces an indefinite/single instance ('one' / 'a certain') modifying دیده‌بان 'watchman, sentinel'. بر سر کوهسار is a locative phrase 'on the summit (crest) of the mountain'; سر is used as 'summit/top' and precedes the place noun کوهسار, forming the usual 'on the top of X' construction.
segments.s259-g124: This is an appositive/descriptor for the sentinel: نگهبان ('guard') qualified by a coordinated object 'روز و ستاره شمار' — literally 'day and star-counter'. شمار functions as a noun 'counting/tally' or agentive sense here (one who counts), so the phrase means 'a guard who keeps track of days and stars' (i.e., keeps constant watch).
segments.s259-g125: شب و روز is a standard adverbial pair meaning 'night and day' (continuous time). گردن برافراخته is a nominal phrase 'neck raised' (i.e., head/neck held high); برافراخته is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'raised/upright', placed after the noun it modifies, which is common in Persian.
segments.s259-g126: از آن دیده‌گه is a locative/source phrase 'from that vantage/lookout'. دیده‌بان ساخته uses ساخته (past participle/verb 'made, set up') with an implied agent from earlier context: 'he made/established (a) sentinel' or 'set a watchman there'. The verb is perfective/past and governs the object (دیده‌بان).
segments.s259-g127: بجستی همی is an archaic/epic perfective with همی giving a continuous or habitual sense in narrative: 'he kept watching/kept peering'. تا introduces the temporal limit 'until', and ز توران سپاه is the genitive phrase 'the army of Turan' functioning as the subject of the following clause.
segments.s259-g128: This clause contains the witnessing verb دیدی ('he saw') and a simile: پی مور ... نهاده به راه. پی here carries the sense 'following / in the wake of' forming a comparative image with مور (ants) — i.e. 'in a line like ants' — while نهاده به راه ('placed/set on the road') describes how they were arranged. The verb دیدی is simple past and نهاده is a perfective participle describing their deployment.
segments.s259-g89: درخشیدن here is a verbal noun (masdar) used as 'the shining' or 'to shine'; it heads a noun phrase that takes تیغهای as its logical subject/possessor (poetic omission of the ezafe). تیغهای is the plural 'swords/blades' with the plural suffix -های; بنفش is an adjective postposed to تیغهای ('purple'). Word order is masdar + (noun phrase) for a nominalized action.
segments.s259-g90: از is the preposition 'from/of'; آن is the demonstrative 'that' (determiner); سایه means 'shadow' or 'shade' and here denotes the shadow/looming presence of the banner; کاویانی is an adjectival/attributive form 'Kayanian' (belonging to the Kayan dynasty) modifying درفش; درفش is 'banner/standard'. Classical poetry often omits the ezafe that would normally link these nouns.
segments.s259-g91: تو گفتی is the simple past 'you said'; که introduces the reported clause ('that'). اندر is an older/prepositional form meaning 'in' (here 'in the night'); شب is 'night'; تیره‌چهر is a compound adjective 'dark‑visaged' or 'sullen/ill‑omened', postposed to شب. The whole segment sets the temporal/location frame for the reported action.
segments.s259-g92: ستاره is the grammatical subject 'the star'; همی is an enclitic/progressive particle in classical Persian indicating continuous or habitual aspect ('kept on' / 'was continually'); برفشاند is a classical present/imperative-form verb meaning 'scatters/throws about' (literally 'casts upon' or 'sprays'); سپهر is the object 'the sky/heavens'. The verb takes سپهر as the thing being scattered-over, so the image is of a star sprinkling light across the heavens.
segments.s259-g93: بیاراست is a past/preterite verb meaning 'he adorned/arrayed' (bi‑ prefix + arast). لشکر is the direct object 'the army'. به سان is a simile 'like/as'; بهشت 'paradise' is the thing the army is compared to. The clause thus says he adorned the army, making it like heaven.
segments.s259-g94: به باغ وفا is a prepositional phrase 'into/at the garden of fidelity' (وفا modifies باغ; ezafe is omitted poetically). سرو کینه is a genitive/attributive sequence 'a cypress of hatred' (سرو = cypress, کینه = hatred/grudge). بکشت is the past form meaning 'he planted/caused to be placed' (classical narrative past); context with 'سرو' and 'باغ' favors the sense 'planted' rather than 'killed'.
segments.s259-g95: فریبزر is a proper name (the recipient). را is the definite object marker placed after the name for emphasis. داد is the past 'gave'. پس here functions as a temporal connective 'then/afterwards'. میمنه means 'the right side/right flank' (often used of a battle line). Notably, Persian poetic syntax often inverts the ordinary prosaic order, so the thing given (میمنه) follows the verb.
segments.s259-g96: پس again is connective 'then'; پشت لشکر is a locative phrase 'behind the army' (پشت + genitive). حصار و بنه is a coordinated pair 'a fortress (حصار) and a ben(h)/heap/earthwork' (و = 'and'). The verb is carried over from the previous clause by ellipsis (classical poetry often omits repeated verbs), so the meaning is that behind the army he set/placed a fortress and a mound/earthwork.
segments.s259-g57: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. شب تیره شد is a complete time clause: شب (night) + تیره شد (became dark), where شد is the past-tense verb 'became'. پیل پیش سپاه is the noun phrase carried over to the next clause (object phrase): پیل = 'elephant' and پیش سپاه = 'in front of / before the army' (پیش here = 'in the presence of, before').
segments.s259-g58: فراز functions as an adverbial 'on high / aloft' modifying the verb آوریدند. آوریدند is past plural 'they brought' and is followed by the coordinating و 'and'. بستند is past plural 'they closed/blocked' and راه (road) is its object; poetry places the object after the verb (بستند راه) rather than the more analytic راه را بستند.
segments.s259-g59: برافروختند is past plural 'they kindled/ignited'. The direct object follows: آتش 'fire'. از هردو روی is a prepositional phrase 'from / on both sides' (از = 'from', هردو = 'both', روی = 'side/surface'), indicating location/origination of the fires.
segments.s259-g60: از introduces the source/cause 'from/at the sound of ...'. آواز here means 'call' or 'shout' and is followed by گردان 'troop/company' (a noun) modified postpositively by the adjective پرخاشجوی 'aggressive' — a typical Persian order: noun then adjective.
segments.s259-g61: جهان is 'the world' and سربه‌سر here is an adverb meaning 'entirely/throughout' modifying the statement. گفتی is the past second-person verb 'you said' introducing reported speech (note: the supplied lemma senses list does not include the verb sense). آهرمنست is the predicate 'is Ahriman' (Ahriman = the evil spirit).
segments.s259-g62: This clause states where the enemy lies using a comparative-like classical construction: به دامن بر از آستین ... دشمنست. به دامن بر (on the hem) بر از آستین (rather than on the sleeve) — بر ... از here functions to contrast two locations. دشمنست is the predicate 'is the enemy'.
segments.s259-g63: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' or 'at' marking cause/source: 'at the cry'. بانگ is 'a shout/cry'. تبیره is problematic in the supplied sense list (given as 'interpretation/explanation') but here functions adjectivally or as a compound with بانگ to qualify the shout (i.e., an ominous/marked cry) — the next phrase به سنگ اندرون ('toward/into the inner stone/place') indicates a motion or impact inward (به = 'toward/into', سنگ = 'stone', اندرون = 'inside').
segments.s259-g64: This clause gives the effect: به درد دل اندر (into the heart's pain) — به marks the result/turning toward, درد دل is the idiomatic 'heartache' (درد = pain, here emotional; دل = heart). اندر is 'in/within', شب قیر گون is a compound adjective phrase 'the tar-black night' (قیر 'tar' + گون forming '-like/-colored'). The whole reads as hearts being afflicted in the pitch-dark night.
segments.s259-g145: سپهدار گودرزشان is a nominal phrase: سپهدار is an old military title 'commander' followed by گودرزشان, Goudarz with the possessive -شان ('their Goudarz'). در میان is a prepositional phrase 'in the midst' (در = in; میان = middle). The phrase places the named commander in the middle of the scene.
segments.s259-g146: درفش is the banner/standard and is followed by a genitive-like phrase introduced by poetic از ('of' here). برش and سایه form a compound describing that banner as 'the (something) of the Kayanian shade' — از is used in classical/epic Persian to mark possession/relationship. کاویان names the Kayanian dynasty; the whole phrase qualifies the standard as connected to the Kayanian 'shade/protection'.
segments.s259-g147: همی is an adverb of continuous or habitual action ('continually/still') modifying بستد (past/preterite form meaning 'bound/tied/fastened'). از ماه و خورشید نور is an object phrase 'the light from the moon and sun' — از is used here in the sense 'from' and نور is the light being bound or gathered in poetic hyperbole.
segments.s259-g148: نگه کرد is a compound verb meaning 'cast/put a look' (نگه = gaze/watch; کرد = did/made) and the usual subject follows (پیران = the elders). به لشکر ز دور is a prepositional phrase 'at the army/from afar' (به = toward/at; ز = from; دور = far). Classical Persian often allows verb + object + subject ordering for emphasis.
segments.s259-g149: بدان here functions like a prefixed prepositional phrase equivalent to 'with that' (ب- as preposition attached to a demonstrative) and ساز is 'tune/instrument' — together 'with that tune'. و links to آن لشکر آراستن: آن is demonstrative 'that', لشکر 'army/host', and آراستن is the infinitive 'to array/adorn' used in classical Persian as the predicative verb ('they arrayed').
segments.s259-g150: دل is the logical object 'the heart'. از introduces the things removed or taken away 'from' the heart: ننگ (shame) و تیمار (worry/care). پیراستن is an infinitive 'to array/attend to' used here idiomatically as 'make/put in a new state' — the poetic meaning is 'to cleanse or free the heart of shame and care.'
segments.s259-g151: This is a linked series of locative nouns introduced by an initial در ('in'): در و دشت و کوه و بیابان lists locations ('in plain and field and mountain and desert'). سنان (spears) follows as the main noun; classical Persian often places location series before the subject or object for vivid listing.
segments.s259-g152: عنان بافته is a past-participle adjective phrase 'reins braided' (عنان = reins; بافته = woven/braided). سر به سر is an idiom 'head to head' or 'one to another' (literally 'head to head'), and با عنان ('with reins') repeats the instrumental relation; the construction creates a tight, repetitive image (reins braided, head to head with reins).
segments.s259-g17: چنین (thus) is an adverb placed before the speech verb to mark manner. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' (poetic past) and is followed by the prepositional phrase با گیو 'with/to Giv' (با used to mark the person addressed/partner). پس پهلوان is an inverted nominal phrase 'then the hero/champion' (پس = then) that functions as the subject in this poetic word order (verb before subject).
segments.s259-g18: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). پیران 'the elders' is the subject; به سیری رسید is a verbal phrase where به + سیری (سیری = satiety/contentment) with رسید 'reached' conveys 'reached contentment'. از روان literally 'from/of the spirit' (روان = soul/spirit) gives the source or scope of that contentment. Word order is subject → verbal phrase → source phrase.
segments.s259-g19: همین 'just/just now' limits the action. داشتم is the past imperfective 'I had / I was keeping' used here as a past-tense first-person auxiliary-like verb. چشم 'eye/eyes' is the object kept hidden; زان 'from that' marks the source (that bad thing) and بد 'bad/ill' modifies it; نهان 'hidden' is a predicative adjective. Together they form 'I had my eyes hidden from that evil.'
segments.s259-g20: ولیکن is a conjunctive adversative 'but/yet' introducing contrast. به فرمان is an instrumental use of به 'by/through' (here meaning 'by the order of') and فرمان 'command/order' names the instrument. شاه جهان is a title in the genitive sense 'king of the world' (شاه = king, جهان = world) attached to فرمان to mean 'by the command of the sovereign.'
segments.s259-g21: ببایست is an impersonal/modal past form meaning 'it was necessary / one had to'. رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' that follows the necessity. که introduces the reason clause 'because/for' and چاره نبود is the explanatory clause 'there was no remedy/option' (نبود = was not). The whole reads 'it was necessary to go, because there was no choice.'
segments.s259-g22: دلش را is the definite object 'his heart' (دل + possessive -ش, plus object marker را). کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb. شهریار is the subject 'the king/sovereign', and آزمود is the past verb 'tried/tested'. Note the poetic inversion: object + adverb + subject + verb, yielding 'now the king tested his heart.'
segments.s259-g23: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'a certain / once' introducing the tale. داستان 'story' is the direct object and گفته بودم is the compound past 'I had told' (گفته + بودم = I had said/told). به شاه 'to the king' marks the recipient. The overall meaning is 'I had once told the king a story.'
segments.s259-g24: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. فرمود is the past verb 'he commanded/said' (here 'ordered'). لشکر کشیدن به راه is an infinitival construction where لشکر 'the army' is the object/actor and کشیدن 'to draw/move' with به راه 'onto the road / to set out' means 'to march the army along the road.' The clause therefore means 'when he ordered the army to march out.'
segments.s259-g185: گر is the conditional 'if' introducing the first possibility. از رزمگه is a locative/ablative phrase 'from the battlefield' (ز/از used poetically). پی نهد is a verb phrase: پی (here 'after, in pursuit / following') combines with نهد (3rd-person singular verb from نهاد 'to place/put' used as 'to set oneself / to go after') to mean 'he moves/steps forward' or 'pursues', while پیشتر is an adverb modifying the verb meaning 'further forward / in front'. Word order is classical: conditional particle → prepositional phrase → verb phrase → adverbial modifier.
segments.s259-g186: وگر ('and/but if') introduces the alternative conditional. جنبد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of جنبیدن 'to move; to stir' meaning 'he moves' or 'he withdraws'. از خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'from himself / from his post' (خویشتن = himself, emphatic), and بیشتر ('more') modifies جنبد to mean 'moves/withdraws even more'. The clause thus gives the second possible motion contrasted with the first.
segments.s259-g187: سپهدار is the subject 'commander'. رویین is a postpositive adjective 'iron‑hard, armored' describing سپهدار. به کردار is a simile/phrase-former 'in the manner/behavior', and شیر (lion) completes the simile: 'in the behavior of a lion' or 'acting like a lion'. The preposition به here marks the manner ('by/‑ly').
segments.s259-g188: پس is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. پشت او is a locative 'behind him' (پشت + pronoun او). اندر آید is a classical locative + verb combination: اندر 'in/into' + آید (3rd‑sg present of آمدن) 'comes', so 'comes up to/into his rear'. دلیر is a final adjectival predicate 'brave', characterizing the commander; in Persian adjectives often follow the clause they modify.
segments.s259-g189: همان is the demonstrative 'that same' (emphatic). دیده‌بان is the noun 'watchman/sentinel' (the subject). بر سر کوه is a locative phrase 'on the mountain's peak' (بر = on; سر = top/summit; کوه = mountain). کرد is the past tense verb 'did / placed / caused' — here meaning he stationed himself or set himself there.
segments.s259-g190: که introduces a resulting or relative clause 'so that/which'. جنگ سواران is a genitive/construct phrase 'the battle of the horsemen' (جنگ = battle; سواران = horsemen). بی‌اندوه is an adjective 'without sorrow / carefree' modifying that battle, and کرد (again past 'made') is the verb indicating the sentinel's action produced that cheerful/carefree state.
segments.s259-g191: This phrase uses inverted/older word order: ز + ایرانیان‌گر + سواری + ز دور corresponds to a conditional 'اگر سواری از ایرانیان از دور' — 'if a horseman of the Iranians, from afar…'. The initial ز (از) marks origin 'from'; ایرانیان‌گر here is an archaic/compounded form indicating 'one of the Iranians' (the -گر element functions in older Persian to form concessive/conditional compounds); سواری is 'rider/horseman'; the final ز دور is 'from afar' (ز = from, دور = distant).
segments.s259-g192: عنان تافتی is a verb phrase: عنان 'the reins' is the direct object/thing handled, and تافتی is the verb (3rd‑sg past of تافتن used here with sense 'turn/bend/whip' → 'he turned/spurred'). سوی is the preposition 'toward', پیکار 'battle/struggle', and تور is the proper name 'Tūr' (a person); together 'turned his reins toward Tūr's battle' or 'spurred toward Tūr's fight.'
segments.s259-g169: گرفتِند (سپرد) is a past transitive verb here (سپرد) placed after the phrase it governs; چپ لشکرش را is the definite object marked by را and means "his left army/wing." بدیشان (به ایشان) is a contracted prepositional phrase meaning "to them/into their charge." Word order is typical poetic Persian: object phrase + indirect object + verb.
segments.s259-g170: This is a noun phrase giving the person and force entrusted: ابا is a proper name here (not the verbal sense in the lemma listing), followed by the numeral سی‌هزار "thirty thousand" and the genitive phrase از دلیران "of the brave." گرد here functions as a collective/force marker (a poetic use meaning "band/troop") rather than the literal senses in the small glosses, so it names the group composition.
segments.s259-g171: چو introduces a comparison or listing here: "like/as." لهاک جنگی is a compound name/epithet (لهاک as a proper name; جنگی as "warlike" or part of the epithet meaning "the warrior"). و simply links the next name فرشیدورد, which is a personal name.
segments.s259-g172: This repeats the formulaic identification: ابا is again a proper name (hence sense -1), followed by the numeral سی‌هزار "thirty thousand." از functions as a partitive/genitive marker here ("of/out of the..."), دلیران is the plural adjective/noun "the brave," and مرد is used as "men/valiant men" — an epic usage emphasizing heroic persons.
segments.s259-g173: گرفتند is the past plural of گرفتن "they seized/took." بر میمنه is a prepositional phrase "on/to the right (hand/side)" (میمنه = right side), and جایگاه is the noun "position/place." The verb comes first (typical poetic order) and the locative phrase follows.
segments.s259-g174: جهان سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning "the whole world/from end to end." گشت is the past of گشتن used in the copular sense "became/turned." ز introduces the cause/agentive source (poetic use of "from/because of"), and آهن سیاه means "black iron" (iron = weapons/steel in poetic diction). The phrase says the place was everywhere covered/turned black with iron.
segments.s259-g175: چو again means "like/as." زنگوله گرد is a compound epithet literally "bell-girt" or "ringed with small bells" (زنگوله = small bell; گرد here is part of the compound meaning "girt/ornamented with" rather than the simple preposition sense), و links the next name کلباد. را after کلباد marks that name as definite/object of the following relative clause or as emphatic.
segments.s259-g176: سپهرم here functions as a proper name (Sepehram). که introduces a relative clause "who/that..."; بد روز is a collocation meaning "a bad/ill day" (بد = bad + روز = day), and فریاد را is the object "the cry/the shout" marked by را. The verb of the clause is omitted in the poetic line and must be supplied in sense (e.g., "he uttered/raised").
segments.s259-g161: پس از + noun = 'after (someone)'. Here پس takes the sense 'after' and is used with از to form a temporal prepositional phrase 'پس از نامداران افراسیاب' = 'after Afrasiab's renowned ones.' Word order is Postposition (پس) + از + noun.
segments.s259-g162: This clause names the actor and his action: کسی‌کش (کسی + کش) functions as an agentive compound 'a man‑killer' (کش as an agentive element). سر از کینه گیرد is literal 'takes a head from hatred' — گیرد is the present/narrative form 'grips/takes' and شتاب (here a noun 'haste') completes the sense 'takes (up) haste' = 'hastens'. In epic Persian this present form is used for vivid, immediate narration.
segments.s259-g163: گزین کرد is a two‑part past tense verb 'he chose' (گزین = choose + کرد = did). The direct object follows the verb: شمشیرزن سی‌هزار = 'thirty thousand swordsmen' (numeral follows the noun in this phrasing).
segments.s259-g164: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that'; بودند is the past plural 'they were'; شایسته = 'worthy/fit'; کارزار = 'battle'. Together 'که بودند شایسته کارزار' = 'who were fit for battle.'
segments.s259-g165: به هومان سپرد = 'he entrusted to Homan' (به + indirect object + past verb سپرد). آن زمان is a time‑phrase 'at that time' inserted parenthetically. قلبگاه is the object 'the heart/center' (of something supplied by the next segment).
segments.s259-g166: قلبگاه سپاهی = 'the heart/center of the army' (سپاهی here is a genitive/attributive 'army' or 'military'). The following words هژبر اوژن و رزمخواه are names/epithets listed as the persons comprising that 'heart' (i.e. the core commanders/warriors).
segments.s259-g167: بخواند اندریمان = 'he called/summoned Andariman' (بخواند here = summon/call in epic present/past narrative usage). و او خواست را = 'and he (او) — the desire/wish (خواست) — (را marks it as definite object)' preparing the object for the next verb.
segments.s259-g168: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put/assigned'. چپ لشکر و راست را = 'the army's left and the right' (چپ = left flank, لشکر = army/host; راست = right flank; را marks the definite object). Together: 'he assigned the left (of the army) and the right.'
segments.s259-g177: برفتند is the 3rd‑person plural past (epic narrative) meaning “they went/advanced.” با is the preposition “with.” نیزه‌ور is a noun/adjective “lancer, spear‑bearer.” Poetic inversion places the numeral after با, so با نیزه‌ور ده هزار = “with ten thousand lancers.”
segments.s259-g178: به here marks direction/toward (“to/against”); پشت means “rear/behind”; سواران = “horsemen.” خنجرگزار is a compound noun “dagger‑bearer” (literal, not the figurative ‘treacherous’ sense). Together به پشت سواران خنجرگزار means “against / to the rear of the dagger‑bearing horsemen.”
segments.s259-g179: رویین and رویینه‌تن are stacked epithets: رویین “iron‑hard” + رویینه‌تن “armored, mail‑clad.” برون‌رفت here functions as a poetic verbal noun/nominal phrase meaning “(they) came forth / came out,” though the provided sense list does not give a verbal sense; the line uses the phrase to announce the emergence of the armored troops.
segments.s259-g180: ابا is a metrical/poetic spelling variant of با (with) here introducing the number; ده هزار از یلان ختن = “ten thousand of the heroes of Khotan.” Note: ختن is a place/people name (Khotan) in context; the provided sense for ختن (circumcise) does not match the toponymic use, so it’s treated as a proper name here.
segments.s259-g181: بدان here is the compound sense ≈ به + آن “to that/into that”; تا introduces extent/purpose (“so that / into that …”). دران بیشه اندر = “in that grove” (دران = in that, بیشه = grove, اندر = in/within). چو شیر is a simile “like a lion,” modifying the actor who follows in the next segment. Word order is poetic; the locative phrase precedes the verb.
segments.s259-g182: کمینگه is the noun “ambush.” کند is the verbal form “does/sets” (here used in the narrative as a simple present/historical present: “sets an ambush”). با introduces the accompaniments: یلان دلیر = “brave heroes.” So کمینگه کند با یلان دلیر = “he sets an ambush with brave heroes.”
segments.s259-g183: طلایه is the vanguard/forerunning force. فرستاد is the simple past “sent.” بر سوی کوه is a motion phrase “toward the mountain” (بر + سوی = toward, کوه = mountain) — a common poetic way to mark direction.
segments.s259-g184: سپهدار ایران = “the commander of Iran” (title + genitival noun). شود is “became” (here narrative/historical present meaning “was/become”), ز + و stands for از او (“from/of him / because of him”) in contracted poetic form, and ستوه = “weary.” The clause means the Iranian commander grew weary/exhausted because of him. (Note: the segmentation shows ز and و separately; together they function as از + او “from him.”)
segments.s259-g193: نگهبان is the subject 'the sentinel/guard'. دیده here is the noun 'eye' used as object in the verbal phrase دیده گرفتن ('to raise/take one's eyes'). گرفتی is an archaic/poetic past-form of گرفتن meaning 'took/raised' (here rendered as 'lifted' his eyes). خروش that follows is a nominal afterthought 'a cry/roar' describing what happened when he looked.
segments.s259-g194: همه 'all' modifies رزمگاه 'the battlefield'. آمدی is a poetic past-form of آمدن 'to come' meaning 'came' or 'burst forth'. ز ... به جوش literally 'from it into boiling' — ز is the poetic 'from' and به جوش 'into a surge/boiling' describes the battlefield's reaction to the cry.
segments.s259-g195: دو لشکر 'two hosts' is the plural subject. به روی ... آورد روی is a doubled-face construction: به روی = 'toward the face/front' and آورد (past of آوردن) = 'brought'; the final روی repeats/echoes 'face/front' — together 'brought their fronts face to face' (i.e., the two armies faced one another). The word order (prepositional phrase + verb + echo noun) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s259-g196: همه 'all' quantifies نامداران 'the renowned ones/renowned warriors'. پرخاشجوی is an adjectival descriptor 'aggressive/quarrelsome' indicating their eagerness to fight; the phrase comments on the character of these named champions as they confront each other.
segments.s259-g197: چنین ایستاده is a participial phrase 'standing thus/so'. سه روز و سه شب is a time phrase 'three days and three nights' following the participle to state the duration (typical Persian order: participle + duration).
segments.s259-g198: یکی را here functions idiomatically as 'not one (of them)' — را is an emphatic/definite marker. به گفتن 'to speaking' (literally 'for saying') introduces the action that did not occur. نجنبید is the negative past/poetic past of جنبیدن 'to move/stir' ('did not move'); لب 'lip' is the item that did not move — together: 'not one moved a lip to speak.'
segments.s259-g199: همی گفت is a habitual/continuous narrative: همی ('continually/still') + گفت ('said') = 'kept saying'. گودرز‌گر is the name/epithet (the speaker). پشت خویش combines پشت 'back' with خوی + ش (the latter two are written separately here) — خویش = 'his own' (the enclitic ش = 'his') so پشت خویش = 'his back.'
segments.s259-g200: سپارم and نهم are first-person verb forms ('I entrust / I place'), here expressing the speaker's resolve or conditional intention; classical/poetic Persian often uses parallel verbs in this way. بدیشان = be + ایشان 'to them' (dative). پای پیش literally 'foot forward' is an idiom meaning 'to step forward' or 'to advance' — so the clause says he will entrust his back to them and step forward.
segments.s259-g201: سپاه اندر آید is a clause with subject then verb: 'the host/army comes in/advances' (آید, 3rd sg. present/future). پس is a connective particle 'then' introducing the result clause that follows; پشتِ من ('my back/rear') is a possessive noun phrase placed after the connective to show what the result will concern.
segments.s259-g202: نماند is used in the sense 'be left / remain' (something will not remain). جز 'except' introduces the sole thing that will remain. از here marks source/what remains 'of/from', باد is literal 'wind' (not optative), در مشت من is a locative/possessive phrase 'in my fist' (مشت من = my fist).
segments.s259-g203: شب و روز is an adverbial phrase 'night and day'. بر پای پیش سپاه is a prepositional/locative phrase: بر 'upon/on', پای پیش 'the forward/foremost step/foot' with سپاه as the possessor/host being 'the army's front'—together 'in front of the army' or 'before the army's vanguard'.
segments.s259-g204: همی is the continuous/imperfective particle meaning 'kept on/constantly'. جست is the past of 'to leap/spring' used poetically ('leapt' / 'kept leaping'). نیک اختر is an epithet 'auspicious/bright star', and هور و ماه are poetic luminous figures (literally 'light/splendor and moon' here functioning like 'sun and moon'), linked by و.
segments.s259-g205: Here the first که is a subordinating/relative particle 'that / when'. روزی functions idiomatically as 'a day / the day' (classical usage 'روزی که' = 'the day when'); the second که again introduces the clause 'that that day is...'. آن روز 'that day' is the noun phrase, and نیک‌اخترست is an adjectival/predicative compound meaning 'is well-starred / auspicious' (نیک‌اختر + است fused poetically).
segments.s259-g206: کدامست is an interrogative 'which is it?'. و 'and' links the next question. جنبش 'movement / maneuver / action' is the thing being asked about. که را is an interrogative 'for whom' (که = who/whom, را marks the definite object/beneficiary), and بهترست is the predicate 'is better / is preferable'. The whole asks which action is best and for whom.
segments.s259-g207: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. بردمد is a verb meaning 'be carried off / be borne' (here transitive/passive sense 'be carried'). باد functions not as mere noun but in an optative/agentive role ('may the wind...'/ 'by the wind') collocated with بردمد. روز نبرد is a genitive compound 'the day of battle' (battle-day) — together asking 'where might the battle-day be carried by the wind'.
segments.s259-g208: که introduces a purposive/subordinate clause 'so that / such that'. چشم سواران is a possessive phrase 'the riders' eyes' (چشم = eye). بپوشد is the subjunctive/optative form of پوشیدن 'to cover' meaning 'may cover / should cover'. به گرد is a prepositional phrase 'with dust' (به = with/by, گرد = dust), so the clause means 'so that it covers the riders' eyes with dust'.
segments.s259-g209: بریشان is a contracted/clitic form meaning 'بر ایشان' = 'against them'; بیابم is first-person singular present/subjunctive (optative) of یافتن, literally 'may I find' / 'I can find'; مگر here is the conditional 'unless' that introduces the exception; دستگاه is a noun 'system/arrangement' used here as 'battle-formation'. Together the segment reads 'unless I find a formation against them'.
segments.s259-g210: به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of' (به + noun meaning 'by/with'), کردار = 'manner/behavior'; باد is literal 'wind' used metaphorically ('swift as the wind'); اندر آرم is a classical first-person verb phrase meaning 'I bring/put in' (اندر = 'into/within', آرم = 1sg verb), and سپاه is the object 'the army'. The phrase means 'I will bring the army in in the manner of the wind' → 'I will bring the army like the wind.'
segments.s259-g211: نهاده is the past participle / perfect form of نهادن ('placed', here 'fixed/turned (one's gaze)'); سپهدار پیران is the noun phrase 'the commander Pīrān' (agent); دو چشم ('two eyes') is the object of the verb, with the numeral preceding the noun. The whole clause means 'Pīrān the commander fixed his two eyes.'
segments.s259-g212: که introduces a result/clause ('so that' / 'such that'); گودرز is the proper name Goudarz and را is the definite object/ topical marker attaching to it; دل is 'heart' and بجوشد is a present/subjunctive form of جوشیدن meaning 'to boil' or 'to seethe (with emotion)'; ز + خشم means 'from/because of anger'. The clause reads 'so that Goudarz's heart seethed with anger.'
segments.s259-g213: کند is a third-person verbal form meaning 'he does/acted' (here narratively 'he turned'); پشت بر دشت is a locative expression 'his back toward the plain' (پشت = 'back', بر = 'on/toward', دشت = 'plain'); و is the conjunction 'and'; راند is the verb 'drove/put to flight' (past/narrative sense); سپاه is 'army/host'. So the clause says 'he turned his back to the plain and drove (the host).'
segments.s259-g214: سپاه اندر آرد is 'he brings/brings in an army' (سپاه = 'army', اندر = 'into/within', آرد = 3sg 'brings'); به پشت سپاه is a directional phrase 'to the rear of the army' (به = 'towards/to', پشت = 'rear/back', سپاه = 'army'), so the full segment specifies that he 'brought troops in at the army's rear.' Note the repetition of سپاه: first as the brought force, then as the host whose rear is meant.
segments.s259-g97: گرازه سر is an epithet formed by noun + سر ('head'), literally 'boar‑headed'. تخمه گیوگان is a genitive/attributive phrase: تخمه 'offspring/line' + گیوگان (the group/name) = 'scion of the Givgan'. Word order is modifier before head (epithet then lineage), so read as 'the boar‑headed (one) of the Givgan.'
segments.s259-g98: This is an appositive identification: زواره is a proper name (Zovāreh) followed by نگهدار 'guardian' describing his office and تخت کیان 'the throne of the Kiyān' (تخت 'throne' + کیان 'the Kayan kings/royal house'). The nouns are in natural order (name → title → object of guardianship).
segments.s259-g99: به یاری is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the help (of)'; فریبرز is the proper name (object of that phrase). برخاستند is a 3rd‑person plural past tense verb 'they rose/advanced'. Together it means 'they rose up to help Fereburz.'
segments.s259-g100: به یک روی is literally 'on one face/side' — here idiomatically 'on a single front/line'. لشکر is the direct object 'the army/host'. بیاراستند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they arrayed/ordered/adorned' (used here as 'they drew up/formed'). Word order is prepositional phrase → object → verb.
segments.s259-g101: به رهام فرمود is 'he said/commanded to Roham' (به + name = 'to Roham'; فرمود is 3rd‑person singular past). پس 'then' links the action; پهلوان is vocative/appellation 'O champion' or a descriptive noun following the address.
segments.s259-g102: که‌ای introduces a clause or vocative 'O you who…' (here addressing Roham). تاج و تخت are copulative nouns 'crown and throne'; خرد را روان places خرد 'wisdom' with را (topical/object marker) and روان as predicate 'spirit'/'soul' — understood as 'you who are crown, throne, and the spirit/soul of wisdom.' The sequence lists titles/qualities applied to the addressee.
segments.s259-g103: برو is the imperative 'go'. با سواران 'with the horsemen' (با + plural noun). سوی is the preposition 'toward'. میسره here names the destination/goal; grammatically it's the object of سوی. The overall phrase is an imperative order to depart with mounted troops toward a place/goal.
segments.s259-g104: نگه‌دار is used as an instructive role 'be (the) guardian/protector'. چنگال گرگ 'the wolf's claw(s)' is a genitive phrase (چنگال + گرگ). از بره 'from the lamb' uses از to mark the source or the one being protected ('keep the wolf's claw away from the lamb'). The clause reads as an order: 'be guardian who keeps the wolf's claw from the lamb.'
segments.s259-g73: پیاده is the noun 'foot soldiers/infantry'. که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a descriptive clause. اندر + خور کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'in/within the خور of the battlefield' (اندر = 'in/within'); the clause is verbless here and qualifies پیاده before the main verb in the next segment — a typical compact relative construction in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s259-g74: بفرمود is an honorific past tense verb 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces purpose here ('in order that / to'). پیش روی is a compound adverbial meaning 'in front of / before' (پیش = before, روی = upon/on). سوار is the target group 'horsemen/ mounted troops'. The implied object of the order is the پیاده from the previous segment.
segments.s259-g75: صفی (here 'a rank/row') is the object/phrase describing what was formed; بر is the verbal prefix (part of برکشیدن) and not a separate preposition in this verbal phrase; کشیدند is 3rd person plural past 'they drew up/formed/drew' and the subject appears after the verb in inverted poetic order: نیزه‌وران ('the spearmen').
segments.s259-g76: This is a continuation of the list of troop-types: گرزداران 'mace-bearers' and کنداوران (an archaic/obscure troop-name). و is the conjunction 'and'. The item ابا in the text is ambiguous/archaic here and likely functions as a connective/variant of با ('with') or an interjection; none of the provided senses fit exactly, so it is left as uncertain.
segments.s259-g77: پیاده marks 'on foot/infantry'; بسی is a classical quantifier meaning 'many/much' (here modifying نیزه‌دار 'spearmen'); the line order places the quantifier and modifiers before the head noun, typical of Persian. همیدون is uncertain/obscure in meaning here (possibly a place-name or poetic adverb) and the edition's senses mark it as unclear.
segments.s259-g78: چه introduces an example/set of particulars here ('some who were with.../such as'), functioning like 'with' to start a list of equipment. با is the preposition 'with'. ترکش is actually the word 'ترکش' (quiver) split in the annotation into ترک + ش; ترک as listed does not match the intended meaning here, so it is marked unknown and ش is the possessive enclitic 'their/its'. و is 'and'. تیر means 'arrow'. جوشن‌گذار is a compound meaning someone who bears or wears a cuirass/mail ('mail‑bearer' or 'armor‑bearer').
segments.s259-g79: کمانها is the plural subject 'the bows'. فگنده is a past participle/adjectival form meaning 'thrown/hung' (here 'hung/slung'). به + بازو درون expresses location 'on/onto the arm' with درون reinforcing 'within/on'. The phrase modifies کمانها and sets up the following verbal clause.
segments.s259-g80: همی is a classical verbal particle marking ongoing or immediate action (here rendering the narrative past). از introduces the ablative 'from'. جگرشان is 'their jigar' (literally 'their livers' or 'their innards/heart-seat'); it is an ablative source. بجوشید is past intransitive 'gushed/boiled forth' (here 'gushed out'), and خون is 'blood' — the clause literally 'blood gushed from their livers.'
